title: Writing - Part 1: Consumerism
description: Your class has been discussing the culture of consumerism and its effects on society and the environment.

content.question: Write an essay discussing two of the effects in your notes. You should explain which effect you consider most harmful, giving reasons for your opinion.

opinions: The constant production and disposal of goods generates huge amounts of waste and pollution.
Many people spend beyond their means to keep up with consumer trends, leading to serious personal debt.
A culture focused on possessions can lead people to prioritise material goods over relationships, creativity and community.

structure.introduction: Introduce consumerism as a defining feature of modern society and its potential downsides.
structure.bodyParagraph1: Discuss the first effect and the harm it causes.
structure.bodyParagraph2: Discuss the second effect and argue which is more damaging.
structure.conclusion: Give your final view on how consumerism should be approached in modern society.

content.modelAnswer:
Consumerism — the constant drive to buy more goods and services — has become a central feature of modern life. While economic activity brings benefits, the culture of consumption also has serious negative consequences. Two of the most damaging are its impact on the environment and its effect on personal finances.

The relentless cycle of production, consumption and disposal generates enormous environmental damage. Factories emit greenhouse gases, products are made from finite resources and millions of tonnes of waste end up in landfill each year. Fast fashion, electronics and single-use plastics are among the most visible examples of how consumer culture is depleting our planet's resources at an unsustainable rate.

Financial debt, however, is a more immediately personal harm. Many people take on credit card debt or loans to maintain a lifestyle they cannot genuinely afford, leading to stress, anxiety and long-term financial insecurity.

In conclusion, while both effects are serious, environmental damage is ultimately the more critical issue because its consequences are global and permanent, affecting not just current consumers but all future generations.
